narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 62.6% chance that you would not pass. This prediction stems from a careful analysis of key elements in your profile, like your undergraduate performance, LSAT score, early grades in school, later academic standing, family income level, and whether you'd be studying full-time. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this concerning prediction were your position in Year 3 grades, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 academic decile. Most influential was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar— this strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of struggling on the exam. Your LSAT score of 39, while above the average of 37 for those who passed, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward success, counterbalancing other concerns. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, again much lower than the typical 6 for passers, contributed toward the failure risk by highlighting early academic challenges. Compared to students who were accepted, who often had stronger decile rankings and undergraduate GPAs around 3.24 versus your 2.70, your profile aligned more closely with patterns seen in those who faced bar exam difficulties. Your upper-middle family income fits well within the common range for successful students, and opting for full-time study matches the overwhelming majority who passed, so those didn't weigh against you. While your undergraduate GPA pulled the odds down a bit, it wasn't among the top drivers here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background— the model focuses purely on bar passage signals from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted support or additional coursework, and perhaps gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth since Year 1. Many students in similar spots have improved their profiles and succeeded— reaching out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies could be your next strong step.
